claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 3 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the age(s) of the child(ren) you're enquiring about, and if possible, their name(s) so I can track each child's eligibility separately.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my two children - Alex, who's 17, and Blake, who's 3 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll assess the eligibility for both Alex (17 years old) and Blake (3 years old) separately.

Let me start by navigating through the decision tree for each child.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good. Now I have questions for each child:

---

**For Blake (age 3):** 
Since Blake is under 16 years old, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. ✓

---

**For Alex (age 17):**
Since Alex is between 16 and 19, I need to ask about their current situation:

1. **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (This includes A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to Level 3, or similar qualifications – but not university/higher education or paid apprenticeships)

2. **Has Alex recently left education or training?** (Within the last few months)

3. **Neither** – Alex is not in education/training and hasn't recently left

Which of these applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment, and he's been out of school for a while now - it's been more than a few months since he left. So I'd say neither of those first two options apply to him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Let me navigate the decision tree for Alex based on this information.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Unfortunately, **Alex does not qualify** for Child Benefit because he is 17 and not in approved education or training.

Now let me continue the assessment for **Blake**. Let me navigate back to check the requirements for a child under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (age 3):**

Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

Given Blake is only 3 years old, I assume the answer is **yes** – Blake is not working. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - Blake is only 3, so he's definitely not working at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (age 3):**

Is Blake **not** receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

Again, given Blake is only 3, I'd expect the answer is **yes** – Blake is not receiving any such benefits. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, Blake doesn't receive any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (age 3):**

Is Blake **not** in any apprenticeship programme?

Given Blake is only 3 years old, I'm confident the answer is that Blake is **not in any apprenticeship**. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - Blake isn't in any apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **Normally live in the UK** – You're ordinarily resident in the UK
2. **Settled status** – You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** – You have pre-settled status and are working/earning above the National Insurance threshold
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** – You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** – You have pre-settled status and can financially support yourself and family (and not claiming certain means-tested benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** – You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as family member** – You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** – You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Do any of these situations apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, you meet the residency requirement. ✓

**Now, are you responsible for Blake?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies:

1. **Living with the child** – Blake lives with you
2. **Contributing to Blake's upkeep** – You pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care (this can include food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** – You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement that cover Blake's upkeep

Which of these applies to your situation with Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're responsible for Blake as he lives with you. ✓

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake?**

- **No** – No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- **Yes, but we've agreed I should claim** – Someone else was/is claiming, but you've agreed between you that you should be the one to claim
- **Yes, and there's a dispute** – Someone else is claiming and you can't agree on who should receive the benefit

Which applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, that's straightforward. ✓

**Where is Blake currently living?**

- **Normal living** – Blake lives at home with you in a normal family arrangement
- **In hospital** – Blake is currently in hospital
- **In care** – Blake is in local authority care
- **Fostered** – Blake is being fostered

Which of these describes Blake's current situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives at home with me in a normal family arrangement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there!

**Final question for Blake:**

Is the local council **NOT** paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance?

In other words, you're supporting Blake yourselves without financial assistance from the council for his care. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards Blake's care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! I've now completed the assessment for both of your children. Let me provide you with the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 📋 Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Final Results

### **Blake (age 3):** ✅ ELIGIBLE

Great news! You **are eligible** for Child Benefit for Blake. He meets all the requirements:
- ✓ Under 16 years old (qualifying child)
- ✓ Not working or receiving benefits
- ✓ You live in the UK
- ✓ Blake lives with you
- ✓ No other claimant
- ✓ Council is not paying for his care

---

### **Alex (age 17):** ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE

Unfortunately, you **are not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex because:
- He is 17 years old and not in approved education or training
- He has been out of education too long to qualify for the 20-week extension period

**Note:** If Alex returns to approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, or NVQs up to Level 3), you may be able to claim Child Benefit for him again until he turns 20.

---

## 📝 Next Steps for Claiming for Blake

1. **Apply online** or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Blake
3. **Provide Blake's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** – if you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

👉 You can apply here: [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

---

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is not currently in approved education or training. He has also been out of education for more than a few months, so he doesn't qualify for the 20-week extension period. For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit is only available if they are in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs up to Level 3) or have recently left and registered with a careers service. Since neither applies, Alex does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16), which means he automatically meets the age requirement. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in any apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), Blake lives with the claimant, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and the local council is not paying towards his care. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for one of their two children. Blake (age 3) meets all eligibility requirements as a qualifying child under 16 living with the claimant. Alex (age 17) does not qualify because he is not in approved education or training and has been out of education for too long to qualify for the extension period.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.reason: No qualifying child under 16, or under 20 in approved education/training
response.guidance: Child must be under 16, or aged 16-19 and in approved education or training. If child just turned 20, benefit stops. If 16-17 year old leaves education, benefit can continue for 20 weeks if they register with careers service or armed services.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

He has also been out of education for more than a few months, so he doesn't qualify for the 20-week extension period. For children aged 16-19, Child Benefit is only available if they are in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs up to Level 3) or have recently left and registered with a careers service. Since neither applies, Alex does not meet the qualifying child criteria.

Blake is 3 years old (under 16), which means he automatically meets the age requirement. He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in any apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), Blake lives with the claimant, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and the local council is not paying towards his care. All eligibility criteria are satisfied.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for one of their two children. Alex (age 17) does not qualify because he is not in approved education or training and has been out of education for too long to qualify for the extension period.
